3A_Bibliographic.citation: Sauce, B., Liebherr, M., Judd, N., & Klingberg, T. (2022). The impact of digital media on children's intelligence while controlling for genetic differences in cognition and socioeconomic background. Scientific Reports, 12, 7720.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Our sample came from the ABCD study (http://abcdstudy.org) and consisted of 9855 participants aged 9-10 years old at baseline and 5169 of these followed up two years later... the baseline when children were ages 9-10 ended with a final sample of 9855 participants (M=9.90 years, SD=0.62 years, 4686 females), and the follow-up two years later when the children were 11-12 years old had a sample of 5374 participants.
3B_Sample_Data.notes: The ABCD data used in this report came from ABCD release 3.0

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Screen time by type: Watching (TV/videos), Socializing (texting, social media, video chat), Gaming (video games)
3C_Exposure.modalities: Watching (TV + videos), Socializing (texting + social networking + video chat), Gaming (video games)
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous (hours/day, means of weekday and weekend)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: The participating children were asked to rate the time they use the following applications on a typical weekday and a typical weekend day: (1) Watch TV shows or movies, (2) Watch videos (e.g., YouTube), (3) Play video games... (4) Text... (5) Visit social networking... (6) Use video chat... We categorized screen time here into three major groups: (a) Watching (1+2), (b) Socializing (4+5+6), (c) Gaming (3).

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Intelligence (latent factor from 5 cognitive tasks) and change in intelligence over 2 years
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Latent factor from: Picture Vocabulary Task, Flanker Task, Oral Reading Recognition Task, Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Task, Little Man Task
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Here we created an index of intelligence based on the remaining tasks and added two new relevant cognitive tasks that were available at both time points. The tasks we used here were: Picture Vocabulary Task, Flanker Task, Oral Reading Recognition Task, Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Task, and Little Man Task.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Latent change score model (strict measurement invariant) with polygenic scores (cogPGS) and SES as exogenous variables; R lavaan package
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: cogPGS (polygenic scores for cognitive performance), SES (composite of income, parent education, neighborhood quality), age, site, 20 genetic principal components
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: We chose to use a latent change score model as it allowed us to examine the effect on intelligence from each type of screen time, cogPGS, and SES simultaneously and with reduced measurement error... We estimated a latent change score model on the change in intelligence from ages 9-10 to 11-12 and containing the predictors (exogenous variables) of cogPGS, SES, and all screen time variables: Watching, Socializing, and Gaming.
3E_Design_Analysis.notes: Used Holm correction for multiple comparisons in Table 1 correlations

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: we found a positive effect on the change in intelligence from screen time Gaming (beta=0.17, p=0.002; or 2.55 IQ points)
we found a positive effect on the change in intelligence from screen time Watching (beta=0.12, p=0.047; or 1.8 IQ points)
screen time Socializing had no independent effect on the change in intelligence (beta=-0.07, p=0.220)

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Screen time Gaming (absolute model, no other screen types)
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Standardized beta from model with Gaming, cogPGS, SES only
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: we found a positive effect on the change in intelligence from screen time Gaming (beta=0.21, p<0.001)

3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: Although seemingly small, the effects we found of screen time on intelligence would still be practically meaningful. Even seemingly small effects can have critical long-run consequences.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Paper does discuss effect sizes substantively (translating betas to IQ points). The gaming effect (beta=0.17-0.21) is moderate by conventional standards.

3H_Recommendations.notes: Paper does not issue clinical or policy recommendations. Discussion is focused on scientific interpretation and future research directions.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion focuses on the novel finding that gaming positively impacted intelligence change over 2 years, consistent with experimental evidence on video game cognitive benefits. It also notes positive effects of watching videos (though less robust). The paper carefully discusses genetic confounding (cogPGS), SES confounding, and the Flynn effect. It appropriately translates standardized betas into IQ-point equivalents. The tone is scientific and does not issue clinical recommendations or overstate findings.
